name: Michigan
notes_normalized: Cannabis is legal in Michigan under state law for recreational adult use. Medical cannabis is also legal under a regulated state program in Michigan. Cannabis remains federally illegal in United States. Possession is limited to varies (see official sources).
notes_raw: style="background:#196E92;" | {{Hs|1}} Cannabis in Michigan is legal for recreational use. State-licensed sales of recreational cannabis began in December 2019. Before cannabis was legalized in the state, possession of any amounts was a misdemeanor offense punishable by up to one year's incarceration and a $2,000 fine, while actual use was punishable by up to 90 days in jail and a $100 fine. Distributing cannabis without remuneration was a misdemeanor punishable by at most one year in jail and a $1,000 fine. The sale and cultivation of cannabis was a felony punishable by up to 15 years imprisonment and $10,000,000 in fines depending on the number of plants grown and the amount of usable cannabis sold. This is because certain prohibitions remain, for example it is still illegal to consume marijuana as the driver of a car, or smoke it as a passenger. Prior to statewide legalization, many cities in Michigan decriminalized cannabis or made enforcement of cannabis laws the lowest priority. Since the 1970s, the college town of Ann Arbor has enacted some of the most lenient laws on cannabis possession in the nation. Before cannabis was legalized in the state, possession of any amounts was a misdemeanor offense punishable by up to one year's incarceration and a $2,000 fine, while actual use was punishable by up to 90 days in jail and a $100 fine. If possession was in a public park, the sentence was at most two years and a $2,000 fine. Distributing cannabis without remuneration was a misdemeanor punishable by at most one year in jail and a $1,000 fine. Prior to statewide legalization, many cities in Michigan decriminalized cannabis or made enforcement of cannabis laws the lowest priority. These include a 1972 city council ordinance, a 1974 voter referendum making possession of small amounts a civil infraction subject to a small fine, and a 2004 referendum on the medical use of cannabis.

state_modifiers.federal_conflict: Cannabis remains federally illegal in United States.
